title: How to Deploy a Dockerized Python Application
subtitle: Standard Operating Procedure for Backend Environments

blocks.text: This guide provides step-by-step instructions for containerizing a standard Python web application using Docker. It strictly follows the Divio documentation framework for practical How-To guides.
Before you begin, ensure you have Docker Desktop and Python 3.12+ installed on your local machine. You will also need access to the internal container registry.
First, create the core application file. We will use a lightweight Flask server for this demonstration.

lines: return f'System is running in {env} mode.'

blocks.text: Always use 0.0.0.0 as the host in your Python script when running inside a Docker container. If you leave it as localhost, the application will not be accessible from the outside.
Next, define the environment and build instructions in your Dockerfile. We strongly recommend using the official python slim images to reduce the final attack surface and image size.

items: Open your terminal and navigate to the project root directory.
Execute the docker build command, tagging your image appropriately.
Run the container and map the internal port 8080 to an available port on your local machine.

lines: # 2. Run the container in detached mode

blocks.text: The following environment variables are supported by the container runtime environment:

rows: Secret key for external API communication.

blocks.text: Never hardcode your API_SECRET in the Dockerfile. Always inject it at runtime using a secure secrets manager or environment variables.

items: Port Conflicts: If port 8080 is already in use, change the mapping in the run command (e.g., -p 9090:8080).
Missing Dependencies: Ensure all required packages are listed in your requirements.txt file before building.
Permission Denied: You may need to prepend commands with sudo on certain Linux environments.

items.text: For advanced multi-stage builds, refer to the official Docker documentation.
The Divio documentation framework separates tutorials, how-to guides, reference, and explanation.
